body.p: Performance OptimizationWhen processing larger amounts of data, there are several options. Either you rely on the performance optimization implemented in the XSLT / XQuery processor, or use methods such as XSLT Streaming - or you go one level lower and see what Java is doing.

It is of course also possible to restructure your algorithms, optimize the code, and make everything (even more) cryptic. But following the motto "Never change a running system", in many places the foundation is fine-tuned first.

section.title: Heap Memory and Garbage Collector
section.p: Performance OptimizationHeap Memory and Garbage Collector If I recall correctly from my computer science studies, a garbage collector automatically collects memory blocks that the Java program (Saxon) no longer needs and hands them over to the heap memory management. A heap is usually an automatically balancing tree that manages the freed memory blocks sorted by size.

Since the garbage collector also runs at the same time as our Java program, the two can get in each other's way. If you have reserved too much heap space for the Java program, then the garbage collector swaps its temporary products to a slow swap area. If you have reserved too little, there is not enough space for the main program.
Common error messages are, for example:
More information on this can be found on the Oracle website on the topic Oracle documentation page on memory error messages

These much-discussed heap settings of the Java Virtual Machine (JVM) have a particularly significant impact on applications where memory consumption at a given time is not predictable, e.g. a Java game with action and occasionally role-playing elements.
In this case, tuning the JVM heap options would be appropriate, especially since you also want to optimize the game for players who do not have access to the most powerful hardware.
Common options that are passed with the Java call are, for example:

p.codeph: Xms<size> set initial Java heap size
Xmx<size> set maximum Java heap size
Xss<size> set java thread stack size

section.p: More information on this can be found on the Oracle website on the topic Oracle documentation page on heap memory settings and garbage collector

For the batch-oriented processes in XML processing, these options are usually not decisive, since the heap is loaded linearly during the transformation of a larger document. In fact, it is even recommended to set the upper limit for heap memory equal to the lower bound, in order to spare the JVM the selection of the appropriate memory area at runtime.
I use a 10 GB heap limit for my performance-intensive transformations:

This allows even very large XML files to be processed efficiently with a pipeline such as MorganaXProc MorganaXProc ToolsMorganaXProc , which keeps the result documents of individual transformation steps in memory.
